[QUERY]: Your task is to revise a scientific text according to given an instruction. The revised text should be in a better state after applying the instructions.

0: The revision is not a better version that can replace the original paragraph in the scientific article.
1: The revision is a better version that should replace the original paragraph in the scientific article.

<START OF EXAMPLE 1>

ORIGINAL TEXT: SuperWeights, which are linear combination of templates which get reused by multiple layers . These SuperWeights capture a single operation on the input features ( e.g ., edge or texture detectors), and are themselves generated via a weighted combination of one or more templates of trainable parameters held by Weight Templates. Thus, to generate the weights for a single layer, we must first construct SuperWeights from the trainable parameters held by Weight Templates (discussed in Section 3.1), and then concatenate together all SuperWeights used by the layer to create its final weights (process illustrated in center-right column of Figure 2).

INSTRUCTION: Make the first half of the paragraph shorter by merging the two sentences and removing the details about how superweights are generated.

ANSWER: SuperWeights, linear combinations of templates reused by multiple layers, capture a single operation on input features (e.g., edge or texture detectors). Generated via weighted combinations of trainable parameters held by Weight Templates, they are then concatenated to create a layer's final weights.

EVALUATION:

<reasoning>The instruction asks to make the first half shorter by merging the two sentences and *removing the details about how SuperWeights are generated*. The provided answer does merge the sentences and shortens the text, but it still includes the generation detail (“Generated via weighted combinations of trainable parameters held by Weight Templates”), which the instruction explicitly requests to remove. Because the revision does not fully follow the instruction and retains disallowed details, it is not an improved replacement for the scientific article. Therefore, the evaluation score should be 0.</reasoning>

<END OF EXAMPLE 1>

[ORIGINAL TEXT]: Line-Entry System) or GNN-based (Graph Neural Networks) MRL methods either take SMILES strings as input that have difﬁculty in encoding molecule structure information, or over-emphasize the importance of GNN architectures but neglect their generalization ability. Here we propose using chemical reactions to assist learning molecule representation. The key idea of our approach is to preserve the equivalence of molecules with respect to chemical reactions in the embedding space, i.e., forcing the sum of reactant embeddings and the sum of product embeddings to be equal for each chemical equation. This constraint is proven effective to 1) keep the embedding space well-organized and 2) improve the generalization ability of molecule embeddings. Moreover, our model can use any GNN as the molecule encoder and is thus agnostic to GNN architectures. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in a variety of downstream tasks, e.g., 17.4% absolute Hit@1 gain in chemical reaction  prediction, 2.3% absolute AUC gain in molecule property prediction,  and 18.5% relative RMSE gain in graph-edit-distance prediction, respectively, over the best baseline method. All experimental code is provided in the supplementary material.

[INSTRUCTION]: Remove unnecessary details on specific numerical performance of the model. Link to https://github.com/hwwang55/MolR instead of supplementary material.

[ANSWER]: Line-Entry System) or GNN-based (Graph Neural Networks) MRL methods either take SMILES strings as input that have difficulty in encoding molecule structure information, or over-emphasize the importance of GNN architectures but neglect their generalization ability. Here we propose using chemical reactions to assist learning molecule representation. The key idea of our approach is to preserve the equivalence of molecules with respect to chemical reactions in the embedding space, i.e., forcing the sum of reactant embeddings and the sum of product embeddings to be equal for each chemical equation. This constraint is proven effective to 1) keep the embedding space well-organized and 2) improve the generalization ability of molecule embeddings. Moreover, our model can use any GNN as the molecule encoder and is thus agnostic to GNN architectures. All experimental code is provided at https://github.com/hwwang55/MolR.

[ORIGINAL TEXT]: There are a lot of measure benchmarking studies considering node classiﬁcation and clustering for both generated graphs and  datasets (Fouss et al., 2012; Sommer et al., 2016; 2017; Avrachenkov et al., 2017; Ivashkin & Chebotarev, 2016; Guex et al., 2018; 2019; Aynulin, 2019a;b; Courtain et al., 2020; Leleux et al., 2020), etc. Although a large number of experimental results, theoretical results  still look unattainable. One of the most important theoretical results for graph measures is a work  Luxburg et al. (2010), where problems of  Commute Time  on big graphs were shown theoretically, and a substantiated amendment was proposed to correct the problem. The paper shows how difﬁcult such proves. Besides difﬁcult proves, there is still no complex empirical understanding of what effects need to be proven. Our empirical work has two main advantages from  previous ones. Firstly, we consider the vast amount of graph measures, which for the ﬁrst time gives the full picture. Secondly, unlike these studies concluding with a global leaderboard, we are looking for the leading measures for each set of  LFR parameters.

[INSTRUCTION]: Use accurate and scientific words.

[ANSWER]: Numerous benchmarking studies have investigated node classification and clustering for both generated graphs and datasets (Fouss et al., 2012; Sommer et al., 2016; 2017; Avrachenkov et al., 2017; Ivashkin & Chebotarev, 2016; Guex et al., 2018; 2019; Aynulin, 2019a;b; Courtain et al., 2020; Leleux et al., 2020), among others. Despite an abundance of experimental results, theoretical outcomes remain elusive. One of the most significant theoretical contributions to graph measures is the work by Luxburg et al. (2010), which theoretically addressed the problems of Commute Time on large graphs and proposed a substantiated amendment to correct the issue. The paper highlights the challenges of providing such proofs. Moreover, there is still no comprehensive empirical understanding of the effects that need to be proven. Our empirical study has two primary advantages over previous ones. Firstly, we examine a vast array of graph measures, providing a comprehensive picture for the first time. Secondly, unlike studies that conclude with a global leaderboard, we aim to identify the leading measures for each set of LFR parameters.

[ORIGINAL TEXT]: The task was created with reference to the previous study [25]. Fig- ure 3 shows a schematic of the task. A pink circular start area (251-pixel radius) and a green target were displayed on a gray back- ground. First, participants clicked on the start area, and the cursor was fixed at the center of the start area. Assuming the initial position of the cursor may affect the cursor path andthe performance of pointing, we strictly fixed a starting position of the trial. Participants clicked again at the starting position, and the trial began. The start area disappeared as a feedback for the beginning of the trial. Partici- pants aimed at the target and ended the trial with the next click. If participants clicked correctly on the target, we marked the trial as a success; else, the trial was marked as a failure (error). We presented a sound feedback in response to the success or failure of the trial.

[INSTRUCTION]: Rewrite the middle part of the paragraph to make it more better. Replace some words in the paragraph.

[ANSWER]: The task was designed with reference to the previous study [25]. Figure 3 illustrates a schematic of the task. A pink circular start zone (251-pixel radius) and a green target were displayed against a gray background. Initially, participants clicked on the start zone, and the cursor was anchored at the center of the start zone. To control for the potential impact of the initial cursor position on the cursor path and pointing performance, we standardized the starting position of each trial. Participants then clicked again at the starting position, and the trial commenced. The start zone disappeared, providing feedback that the trial had begun. Participants directed their cursor towards the target and concluded the trial with their next click. If participants successfully clicked on the target, the trial was deemed a success; otherwise, it was marked as a failure (error). We provided auditory feedback in response to the trial's outcome, indicating success or failure.

It replaces words like "displayed on" with "displayed against," "start area" with "start zone," "fixed" with "anchored," "began" with "commenced," and "aimed at" with "directed their cursor towards." The revised text is more concise and professional. It also clarifies the rationale behind standardizing the starting position. The overall revision is a significant improvement and suitable to replace the original paragraph. Therefore, the evaluation score should be 1.</reasoning>
<score>1</score>
